findings.description: yaml.load(f) is called without a SafeLoader, allowing arbitrary Python object construction / code execution from an attacker-controlled config file.
Merge order applies env then file (file last), so the actual precedence is file > env > defaults, contradicting the documented env > file > defaults contract.
get() calls load() on every invocation, re-reading the file from disk and re-scanning all environment variables with no caching/memoization.
feature_enabled assumes feature_flags is a dict, but an env override (APP_FEATURE_FLAGS) or a non-dict file value yields a string/scalar, causing AttributeError on .get().
Malformed YAML raises yaml.YAMLError which is uncaught, so parse failures crash while a missing file is gracefully handled, an inconsistent error contract.
_coerce aggressively converts numeric-looking strings (e.g. "007", "1_000", padded ints) so values meant to stay strings are silently mangled into ints/floats.
Env keys are lowercased and flattened, so values like APP_FEATURE_FLAGS overwrite structured dict defaults with scalars and nested keys cannot be expressed.
os.path.exists guard before open is TOCTOU-prone and permission/IO errors during open propagate uncaught despite the missing-file case being handled.
Boolean coercion only recognizes "true"/"false"; common forms like yes/no/on/off/1/0 are not treated as booleans, an ambiguous contract.
Logger is created with a hardcoded name "config" instead of __name__, which is non-idiomatic and breaks per-module logger hierarchy.
Helper methods _read_file, _read_env, and _coerce lack docstrings while load/get/require have them, an inconsistency with documentation standards.
